naturalMeaning: The standard reply is simply 'Tudo bem' or 'Tudo' — it's a greeting, not a real health question.

examples.text: Oi, tudo bem? — Tudo, e você?
examples.translation: Hi, how are you? — Good, and you?

examples.text: Uma água sem gás, por favor.
examples.translation: A still water, please.

naturalMeaning: Use it to pass through a crowd, interrupt politely, or enter/leave a room.
In casual speech you'll also hear 'Quanto que é?' or 'Quanto tá?'

examples.text: Desculpa, não entendi. Pode repetir?
examples.translation: Sorry, I didn't understand. Can you repeat that?

examples.text: Esse é o Rafael. — Muito prazer!
examples.translation: This is Rafael. — Nice to meet you!

naturalMeaning: 'Até mais' is an equally common variant.
Casual goodbye when there's no fixed next meeting.
Standard Friday goodbye at work and in shops. Reply: 'Pra você também!'
Very common casual greeting among friends; often just acknowledged with another 'E aí'.

examples.text: E aí, beleza?
examples.translation: What's up, all good?

naturalMeaning: Works as a greeting ('Beleza?'), an answer ('Beleza!') and an 'okay, deal' all in one.

examples.text: Te busco às oito, beleza?
examples.translation: I'll pick you up at eight, cool?

translation: Don't mention it! / Not at all!
naturalMeaning: The warm Brazilian response to 'obrigado' or an apology — it waves the thanks away.

examples.text: Obrigada pela carona! — Imagina!
examples.translation: Thanks for the ride! — Don't mention it!

naturalMeaning: Confusing but common: despite the 'não', it's how attendants offer help. As a reply, 'Pois não!' means 'of course'.

examples.text: Pois não? — Queria ver aquela camisa, por favor.
examples.translation: How can I help? — I'd like to see that shirt, please.

translation: Make yourself at home. / Feel free.

examples.text: Entra, fica à vontade!
examples.translation: Come in, make yourself at home!

naturalMeaning: The natural way to order in Brazil: 'Vou querer o frango com arroz.'

examples.text: Vou querer uma caipirinha e uma porção de fritas.
examples.translation: I'll have a caipirinha and a portion of fries.

naturalMeaning: You can also just make eye contact and mime signing in the air — universally understood in Brazil.

examples.text: Pode trazer o cardápio, por favor?
examples.translation: Could you bring the menu, please?

naturalMeaning: Agrees with the item: 'mais uma' for feminine nouns (mais uma cerveja).
The standard answer to 'Mais alguma coisa?' (Anything else?). Women say 'obrigada'.
'Pra comer aqui ou pra levar?' is the question; 'pra viagem' or 'pra levar' both mean to-go.
The compliment every Brazilian cook wants to hear. Stronger: 'Tá uma delícia!'
The follow-up question will be 'crédito ou débito?' (credit or debit?).
Pix (instant transfer) is accepted almost everywhere, including street vendors.

examples.text: Aceita Pix? — Aceito, a chave é o meu telefone.
examples.translation: Do you take Pix? — I do, my key is my phone number.

naturalMeaning: Standard question for any bigger purchase; the answer is usually 'em quantas vezes?' (in how many installments?).

translation: How much did it come to?
naturalMeaning: What you ask at the register when the total is being rung up.
'Ficar' is the natural verb for locations: 'Onde fica a estação?'

examples.text: Onde fica o ponto de ônibus mais perto?
examples.translation: Where is the nearest bus stop?

examples.text: Como eu chego no centro?
examples.translation: How do I get downtown?

examples.text: Fica longe daqui? — Não, dá pra ir a pé.
examples.translation: Is it far from here? — No, you can walk there.

naturalMeaning: You mostly need to understand this one. Left is 'à esquerda'; straight ahead is 'reto' or 'direto'.

examples.text: Vira à direita na esquina e segue reto.
examples.translation: Turn right at the corner and keep going straight.

naturalMeaning: Only for answering the phone — never as a face-to-face greeting.

examples.text: Tô entrando numa reunião, te ligo depois.
examples.translation: I'm going into a meeting, I'll call you later.

naturalMeaning: In practice this means 'message me on WhatsApp' — SMS is basically dead in Brazil.
'Caiu' is also used for internet going down: 'a internet caiu'.

examples.text: De onde você é? — Sou dos Estados Unidos, mas moro aqui.
examples.translation: Where are you from? — I'm from the US, but I live here.

examples.text: Oi! Quanto tempo! Como você tá?
examples.translation: Hi! Long time no see! How are you?

examples.text: Não vou mais. — Como assim? Você prometeu!
examples.translation: I'm not going anymore. — What do you mean? You promised!

examples.text: Você vai no churrasco? — Com certeza!
examples.translation: Are you going to the barbecue? — For sure!

naturalMeaning: Soft agreement — enthusiastic-neutral. Also handy when ordering: 'Pode ser esse mesmo.'

examples.text: Pizza hoje? — Pode ser!
examples.translation: Pizza tonight? — Sure!

examples.text: Ele vem hoje? — Acho que sim.
examples.translation: Is he coming today? — I think so.

naturalMeaning: Shrug in verbal form. Fine among friends; too dismissive for formal settings.

examples.text: Que horas ele chega? — Sei lá, ele nunca avisa.
examples.translation: What time does he arrive? — Dunno, he never says.

examples.text: Desculpa, pode repetir mais devagar?
examples.translation: Sorry, can you say that again more slowly?

translation: How do you say... in Portuguese?

examples.text: Como se diz 'schedule' em português?
examples.translation: How do you say 'schedule' in Portuguese?

examples.text: O que significa 'saudade'?
examples.translation: What does 'saudade' mean?

naturalMeaning: Magic phrase — Brazilians will slow down, help you, and usually compliment your Portuguese.

examples.text: Desculpa, tô aprendendo português ainda.
examples.translation: Sorry, I'm still learning Portuguese.

naturalMeaning: All-purpose 'let's do it': as a question it proposes, as an answer it accepts.

examples.text: Bora pegar uma praia amanhã? — Bora!
examples.translation: Wanna hit the beach tomorrow? — Let's go!

naturalMeaning: Warm but often noncommittal — the Brazilian 'we should hang out sometime'. Pin a date if you mean it.

examples.text: Vamos marcar um café qualquer dia!
examples.translation: Let's grab a coffee sometime!

examples.text: Sexta às oito, então? — Combinado!
examples.translation: Friday at eight, then? — Deal!

examples.text: Que horas a gente se encontra?
examples.translation: What time are we meeting?

examples.text: Churrasco no domingo, quem topa? — Tô dentro!
examples.translation: Barbecue on Sunday, who's up for it? — I'm in!

translation: I won't be able to make it.

examples.text: Que pena, não vou poder ir no sábado.
examples.translation: What a shame, I won't be able to make it on Saturday.

translation: No, thank you. (said by a man)
naturalMeaning: Women say 'Não, obrigada.' Polite and final — no explanation needed.
'Dar' as 'to be possible' — the softest everyday refusal.

examples.text: Hoje não dá, mas amanhã tô livre.
examples.translation: Today doesn't work, but tomorrow I'm free.

literalMeaning: it stays for the next one

examples.text: Não consigo hoje. Fica pra próxima?
examples.translation: I can't today. Rain check?

translation: I already have plans / a commitment.
naturalMeaning: The polite all-purpose excuse; 'compromisso' covers anything from a meeting to a date.
The emergency shout. Informally also hyperbole, like 'OMG help'.

examples.text: Me ajuda, por favor, me perdi.
examples.translation: Please help me, I'm lost.

naturalMeaning: The ambulance number in Brazil is 192 (SAMU); police is 190.

examples.text: Perdi minha carteira com todos os documentos.
examples.translation: I lost my wallet with all my documents.

naturalMeaning: Women say 'Tô perdida.' Also figurative: lost/confused in a conversation.

examples.text: Tô perdido, você sabe onde fica essa rua?
examples.translation: I'm lost, do you know where this street is?

translation: Can I buy you a drink?
naturalMeaning: Direct but polite bar opener; works the same as in English.
Warm goodbye after meeting someone new; add 'a gente se fala' to signal you want to stay in touch.

examples.text: Adorei te conhecer! A gente se fala.
examples.translation: I loved meeting you! Let's keep in touch.

naturalMeaning: The standard way to exchange contacts in Brazil — nobody asks for a phone number, everyone asks for 'o zap'.

examples.text: Me passa seu WhatsApp? Te chamo pra gente combinar.
examples.translation: Can I get your WhatsApp? I'll message you so we can set something up.

translation: Want to go out one of these days?
naturalMeaning: Low-pressure invitation; 'sair' can be friendly or a date depending on tone.
The classic corny pickup line, same as in English — use it with a wink, it always gets a laugh.

examples.text: Te respondo depois, tenho uma reunião agora.
examples.translation: I'll get back to you later, I have a meeting now.

translation: Can you send it to me by email?
I'll get back to you later today.
naturalMeaning: 'Retornar' is standard office-speak for returning a call or message.

examples.text: Vou verificar e te retorno ainda hoje.
examples.translation: I'll check and get back to you later today.

examples.text: Semana que vem não vou estar — estou de férias.
examples.translation: I won't be in next week — I'm on vacation.

translation: Let me know if you need anything.
naturalMeaning: The universal helpful sign-off — among friends, coworkers, hosts. 'Qualquer coisa' = 'if anything comes up'.

examples.text: Vou indo. Qualquer coisa, me avisa!
examples.translation: I'm off. Let me know if anything comes up!
